original text:
#414:Beau Goorey/LA Watch Works Baselworld 2018 "Handmade"
today's Handmade episode LET THERE TALK friend the Watch Master himself Beau Goorey LOS ANGELES WATCH WORKS stops and discuss our favorite releases from this years Baselworld 2018.
This one for all you Watch Nerds out there.
talk Rolex,Patek Philippe,Omega and Tudor.
you love watches and all things handmade tune asap.
Today LET THERE TALK friend comedian Jim Florentine stops back talk all things Rock and Comedy.
Jim and talk about his brand new book called "Everybody Awful Except You" its out right now get out there and pick this funny book right away.
#412:Tommy Gelinas Valley Relics Museum "Handmade"
today's episode LET THERE TALK sit down with friend Tommy Gelinas owner the Valley Relics Museum.
The Valley Relics Museum hands down one the coolest places i've ever been. You wanna see collection all things cool this the spot. It's eclectic mix all things Valley like BMX,FAST FOOD SIGNS,ROCK ROLL,MALIBU GRAND PRIX,THE BAD NEWS BEARS and even Nudie Suits.
This episode was much fun and think you will really dig this conversation.
today's episode LET THERE TALK guest the Guitar Legend George Lynch aka Scary.
George stops and gives in-depth interview all things George Lynch.
talk Living,Music Biz and course Dokken and Lynch Mob.
George has been long time dream guest and was very fired finally have this man the show.
Also this podcast brought you Spotify. You can now hear LET THERE TALK and other podcast Spotify.
and talks and growing Sacramento.
"All Things Must Pass: The Rise and Fall Tower Records"
Band Brothers,Dexter and Fargo.
Use the link for 10% off all your nutritional needs like Alpha Brain New Mood and favorite Joint Oil.

original text:
#AusELT privileged and delighted able welcome Scott Thornbury our first Twitter chat guest the Year the Horse. One Scott's current research interests the "ed tech" phenomenon that has dominated ELT recent years, and exploring what this means practical terms. will joining Thurs 6th Feb 8.30pm Sydney time [click here check the time elsewhere the world] discuss some the questions raises below we'll see you there for what promises very discussion.
long ago 1966, Pit Corder warned that 'the use mechanical aids the classroom justified only they can something which the teacher unaided cannot do, can less effectively' (1966, 69). Nevertheless, the craze for newer, better gadgetry has continued unabated, creating what one writer called 'the caravan effect': metaphor which the travellers (technology enthusiasts) stop for while drink from the waterhole (the latest technology) until they have had their fill; then they move the next waterhole drink again' (Levy 2009, 779). Moreover, each innovation arrives garlanded with claims that are seldom ever realised, such that the history educational technology the 20th century has been characterised continuous cycle 'hype, hope, and disappointment' (Selwyn 2011, 59). Why this? One reason (adduced Selwyn) that the power technology often enlisted order solve problems that are nature, language learning being prime example. accept the ecological view that language complex dynamic system, subject multiple and interconnected influences, social, psychological and environmental, the idea that change can effected quick technological fix ingenuous, say the least. The history the social sciences littered with the unintended consequences such interventions. Beware geeks bearing gifts!
order guard against the hype, any recommendation for integrating learning technology into our current practice should countered with Neil Postman's oft-cited riposte: What the problem for which this technology the solution? which might added second question, based Pit Corder's aforementioned warning: Can the technology better/more effectively than the teacher unaided?
The input problem, i.e. how does the learner obtain sufficient (comprehensible) input?
The output problem, i.e. how the learner provided with opportunities for (pushed) output?
The interaction problem, i.e. how does the learner engage (scaffolded) interaction?
The feedback problem, i.e. how does the learner get optimal feedback the point need?
The motivation problem, i.e. what motivates the learner make best use these input, output, interaction and feedback opportunities?
The data problem, i.e. how does the learner readily access useable information about the target language?
It's contention that technology (meaning here 'digital technology', and especially that which available online) has made significant advances terms helping solve least some these problems, such the input problem and especially the data problem, where easily outperforms the unaided teacher. But has some way terms the output, interaction and feedback problems, while the evidence with regard motivation inconclusive.
would further, though, and add that one the unintended consequences uncritical commitment educational technology might the effective disempowering teachers the interests servicing the neoliberal 'knowledge economy'. Lin (2013) warns: 'Language teaching increasingly prepackaged and delivered were standardised, marketable product [...] This commodifying ideology language teaching and learning has gradually penetrated into school practices, turning teachers into 'service providers.' The invisible consequence that language learning and teaching has become transaction teachers passing marketable set standardised knowledge items and skills students.' This commodification process is, course, massively expedited digital technologies.
the early 19th century group English textile artisans aggressively resisted the threat their jobs and lifestyles posed the development new technologies. They were known Luddites. Ever since, the term has been used disparage anyone who questions the assumption that technological innovation always beneficial. But were the Luddites wrong?
Levy, 2009. Technologies use for second language learning. The Modern Language Journal, Focus Issue: Technology the Service Language Learning.
Lin, 2013. Toward paradigmatic change TESOL methodologies: Building plurilingual pedagogies from the ground up. TESOL Quarterly, 47,
Pit Corder, 1966. The Visual Element Language Teaching. London: Longman.
Selwyn, 2011, Education and Technology: Key Issues and Debates. London: Continuum.
This entry was posted EdTech, Professional development and tagged #AusELT, #AusELT Twitter chat, tech, edtech, EFL, ELT, ESL, Mouse That Roared, Scott Thornbury, teaching with technology January 15, 2014 #AusELT.
